question: Explain the basic fetch-decode-execute cycle of a CPU, describing the role of key registers like the Program Counter (PC) and the Instruction Register (IR).
expectedPoints: PC holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched.
Instruction is fetched from memory location in PC and loaded into the IR.
PC is incremented to point to the next instruction.
Control unit decodes the instruction in the IR.
ALU and other components execute the decoded instruction.

question: Compare and contrast a Reduced Instruction Set Computer (RISC) and a Complex Instruction Set Computer (CISC) architecture. Provide two key characteristics of each.
expectedPoints: RISC: Focus on simple, single-cycle instructions.
RISC: Larger number of general-purpose registers.
CISC: Focus on complex, multi-cycle instructions that do more work.
CISC: More specialized instructions that act as high-level abstractions (e.g., a single instruction for a string copy).
RISC: Relies more on compiler optimization to sequence simple instructions.
CISC: Hardware is more complex to decode and execute variable-length instructions.

question: Define the term 'opcode' and explain its purpose within a machine instruction.
expectedPoints: Opcode is the part of a machine instruction that specifies the operation to be performed.
It is a binary code that the control unit decodes.
It tells the CPU what action to take (e.g., ADD, LOAD, JUMP).
The opcode determines what the remaining bits in the instruction format represent (e.g., registers, memory addresses, immediate data).
